coredata.dc:title: Polymer dots loaded fabric as quenching florescence sensor for selective detection of gold
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Alenazi, Duna A.K.
coredata.dc:description: Polymer dots (PDs) as a type of carbon-based nanomaterials are characterized with excellent photoluminescence. Gold is known to be very reactive and potentially noxious for human being. Herein, an investigative methodology is demonstrated for exploiting acrylate wastes for clustering of florescent PDs to be sequentially applied as optical sensors for detection of gold ions (Au3+) in aquatic media. The synthesized PDs were successively immobilized within cationized cotton fabrics that acted as a supporting scaffold to prepare PDs@Fabric for sensitive and selective florescent detection of Au3+ to be commercialized with accurate and satisfactory results. Florescent PDs (5.6 ± 2.1 nm) with zetapotential ranged in −6.5, were successively immobilized within cellulose matrix of cationized cotton for preparation PDs@Fabric to act as quenching florescence scaffold for detection of Au3+ in aquatic media. The florescence response of PDs@Fabric was linear with respect to Au3+ concentration over range of 5 mM to 60 mM with a regression coefficient (R 2) of 0.9895 at low concentrations (5–30 mM), owing to the static quenching mechanism. Meaning that, the fluorescence of PDs was quenched by Au3+ due to chemical coordination between Au3+ and the decorative groups on the surface of PDs, indicating their excellent selectivity to Au3+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acrylate wastes
Polymer Dots
PDs@Fabric
Fluorescence sensor
Gold ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the synthesized PDs and the corresponding particle size distribution. [b] particle size from zetasizer and [c] zeta potential.

Optical properties for the synthesized PDs; [a] Absorbance spectra, [b] Fluorescence emission spectra (excitation at 370 nm & 400 nm), [c] Absorbance spectra after 4 months and [b] Fluorescence emission spectra (excitation at 370 nm) after 4 months.
[a] FTIR spectra and [b] XRD analysis for the synthesized PDs.
NMR spectra for the synthesized PDs; [a] 1H NMR and [b] 13C NMR.
SEM images and EDX analysis for PDs@fabric; [a] cotton fabric, [b] cationized fabric and [c] PDs@fabric.
[a] Fluorescence emission spectra (excitation at 370 nm) for PDs@fabric before and after metal salt treatment and [c] emission intensity at 486 nm (maximum wavelength).

[a] Fluorescence emission spectra (excitation at 370 nm) for PDs@fabric at different gold concentration, [b] emission intensity at 486 nm (maximum wavelength) and [c, d] detection limit of gold (lower & higher concentration).

head.ce:title: Polymer dots loaded fabric as quenching florescence sensor for selective detection of gold

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Tabuk, Tabuk 71491, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Tabuk, Tabuk 71491, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer dots (PDs) as a type of carbon-based nanomaterials are characterized with excellent photoluminescence. Gold is known to be very reactive and potentially noxious for human being. Herein, an investigative methodology is demonstrated for exploiting acrylate wastes for clustering of florescent PDs to be sequentially applied as optical sensors for detection of gold ions (Au3+) in aquatic media. The synthesized PDs were successively immobilized within cationized cotton fabrics that acted as a supporting scaffold to prepare PDs@Fabric for sensitive and selective florescent detection of Au3+ to be commercialized with accurate and satisfactory results. Florescent PDs (5.6 ± 2.1 nm) with zetapotential ranged in −6.5, were successively immobilized within cellulose matrix of cationized cotton for preparation PDs@Fabric to act as quenching florescence scaffold for detection of Au3+ in aquatic media. The florescence response of PDs@Fabric was linear with respect to Au3+ concentration over range of 5 mM to 60 mM with a regression coefficient (R 2) of 0.9895 at low concentrations (5–30 mM), owing to the static quenching mechanism. Meaning that, the fluorescence of PDs was quenched by Au3+ due to chemical coordination between Au3+ and the decorative groups on the surface of PDs, indicating their excellent selectivity to Au3+.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold is known as a highly valuable element in the world’s economy and industrialization, as it is highly exploited in catalysis for material production and as a commodity in manufacturing of accessories, jewelry, and coins. Gold is an abundant element that is naturally occurred in many forms [1]. One of its forms is the existence in nano-dimension, as it is reported to be applicable in the delivery of drug agents [2–6], a common and essential ingredient in cosmetics [7], in catalysis for industrial production [8–11], as a biomarker [12–16] and as a material for connecting elements in electronic chips [17–19]. The numerous applications of gold have led to the aggregation of dangerous wastes such as gold-mining post-production wastes and electronic wastes [20], that containing leftover gold, resulting in water pollution to be harmful for the living organisms.

Gold ions are known to be very reactive and potentially noxious for human being, whereas, it can chemically interact with human biopolymers, especially proteins and deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), that could deteriorate kidneys, peripheral nervous system, and liver [21,22]. Therefore, it is importantly demanded to investigate techniques for detection of gold ions in the environmental and biological systems. Mostly, the detection of gold ions is performed via different traditional methods, that involving complex steps and the application of large and expensive instrumental tools. Some methods are based on the chromatography, and others that exhibit low quantitative accuracy, including inductive coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometric method (ICP-AES) [23], atomic absorption spectrometric method (AAS) [24–26], and the electrochemical analysis [27,28]. In contrast, fluorescence spectrometric technique could be ascribed as a better sensing procedure for gold ions, to be advantageous with low in cost, high sensitivity and selectivity [1].

Numerous reports demonstrated systematic studies for the exploitation of florescent sensors in detection of different pollutants in aquatic streams [29–31]. One the other hand, polymer-based devices could be desirably processed [32–35]. Polymer-based sensing devices have considerably attracted the interest of researchers, that is evident from the number of recently represented researching articles in literature [36]. Sensing devices derived the information about the environments where they are exploited (locally/remotely). Polymer-based devices could be tuned via an appropriate synthetic method or modification technique, reflecting in the polymers to be prominent as sensors [37] with diverse applications [38]. As the most considerable issue is the investigation of costless sensing reagents via the exploitation of an ecofriendly approach. One of the most promising techniques for sensors and biosensors fabrication are polymer-derived techniques. The common polymeric matrices applied in sensing objects are composites, nanocomposites hydrogels, molecular imprinting polymers, and conducting polymers [39–42]. The chemical composition of such polymeric matrices be exploited as sensors could improve the recognition of the target molecules as a basic support for impregnation of different functionalization (dyes metal, nanofillers…etc.,) and by changing the chemical/physical characters, hence promoting a target analyte estimation [43,44]. Another advantage for exploitation of polymer-based sensing templates is the opportunities for the modification of their chemical properties in such a pathway that their reactivity, resistance for degradation, flexibility and biodegradability are changed [45].

Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most significant family of carbon nanostructures owing to its excellent optical activity, photostability, low toxicity, biocompatibility and high affinity for surface decoration. Whereas, numerous reports in literature were recently considered with investigation of quite simple methods for synthesis of CDs to employ in different applications [46–50]. On the other hand, polymer dots (PDs) is a type of zero-dimensional nanostructures with a mean size less than ten nanometer [51]. Investigation of PDs has considerably interested with the researchers attributing to their unique properties such as excellent photoluminescence [52], biocompatibility with low toxicity [53]. Owing to their unique characters, they were widely applied in drug delivery & antimicrobial/anticancer [54,55], bioimaging [56], electrocatalysis [57], photocatalysis [58], photovoltaic [59], sensing [60,61] and energy storage / conversion [62]. In particular, well-known process in photoluminescence like energy transfer, fluorescence quenching, and the sensation of fluorescent spectra for molecular precursor, all points to the wide-scaled applicability of PDs in chemical sensing and biosensing [53].

There are two functional fractions for the sensor device, a recognition fraction for binding to a target analyte and a transducing fraction for signaling the reaction [63]. The current study demonstrates a quite easy, ecofriendly and cost-effective method for synthesis of florescent PDs from acrylate wastes without any of toxic chemicals. The synthesized PDs were successively immobilized within cationized cotton fabrics that acted as a supporting scaffold to prepare PDs@Fabric as a promising florescent dipstick to be easy applicable for efficient, sensitive, rapid and selective florescent detection of gold ions with accurate and satisfactory results. Herein, an investigative methodology is demonstrated for exploitation of acrylate wastes in clustering of florescent PDs to be sequentially exploited as optical sensors for detection of gold ions. The currently reported methodology can be described as a simple and environmentally benign technique for reusing of acrylate wastes to prepare fluorescent PDs. According to our knowledge, no researching reports in literature were concerned with affirming the affinity of PDs ingrained from acrylate wastes to be exploitable as optical sensors for detection of gold ions. In addition to, nucleation of PDs is successively performed without using any of toxic solvents, to be suitable for application in sensitive detection potency. Clustering of PDs via the exploitation of acrylate wastes was approved via different instrumental characterizations like, UV–Visible spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy, FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. Moreover, PDs were successively immobilized within cationized cotton to prepare PDs@Fabric to prepare easily applicable quenching florescence scaffold for detection of gold ions in aquatic streams. The as-prepared PDs@Fabric was well characterized via SEM, EDX & FTIR, whereas, its affinity to be applicable as quenching florescence scaffold was approved via systematic study.

Acrylate wastes were collected from FPC – Coated Technical Textiles in Jeddah – Saudi Arabia. N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF, 99.8%), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%), 3-Chloro-2-hydroxypropyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (69%, commercial name CR-2000 and known as Quatt-188) were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany and applied as received. Bleached cotton fabrics were purified by washing at 100 °C for half hour by using Na2CO3 (2 g/L) in presence of non-ionic surfactant (1 g/L). Fabrics were rinsed with hot water then with cold water and finally dried at room temperature.

Acrylate wastes (2 g) were cut into small species and immersed in DMF (100 mL) and the mixture was left to 12 h under magnetic stirring for complete dispersion. The reaction pf mixture was raised to 100 °C for 30 minutes and cooled down. Then the mixture was transferred to autoclave vessel and connected to the infrared machine at 125 ℃ “Infra-Red Dyeing Machine” for five hours. After cooling, the samples were centrifuged (Hermile Z326 K) for 20 minutes at 4000 rpm for the separation of dispersed particles. Additionally, the sample colloids were dialyzed with “pure-A-lyzer with 6–8 kDa kits (Sigma-Adrich)” to obtain the purified PDs.

Cotton fabrics were functionalized by cationization process via the interaction with 3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl trimethyl ammonium chloride as quaternary ammonium salt as reported in literature [64]. Fabrics were firstly activated by alkalization, though impregnation of fabric (5 gm) in 250 mL of NaOH (1 M) for 20 minutes. Consequently, fabrics were removed and squeezed to remove the excess of NaOH. Secondly, the wet alkalized fabrics were submerged in 250 mL 3-chloro-2-hydroxypropyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (2 M) for 10 minutes followed by squeezing and the process was repeated to get more efficient interaction. Fabrics were dried at 50 °C for 15 min and thermally cured at 125 °C for 5 min. Finally, the functionalized cotton fabrics were rinsed with distilled water and then dried at 70 °C. Fabrics were cut into small strips with dimension of 2 × 4 cm and then dipped in the synthesized PDs. After 10 min, the PDs@fabrics were taken out and dried at 70 °C prior to analysis and application.

Topography and size average of the nucleated PDs were determined from samples micrographs obtained from “JEOL-JEM-1200 TEM (High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy, Japan)”. Size distribution of PDs was determined with “4-pi analysis software”. Particle size and “zeta potential” were both measured for the synthesized PDs by using Malvern “Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments Ltd – from UK”. The detection was performed within isolated chamber at room temperature using “dynamic light scattering, DLS”. The absorbance for the clustered PDs was detected with “Cary 100 UV–VIS spectrophotometer at 250–750 nm”, from Agilent. Spectrometer “Jasco FT/IR 6100″ was applied for obtaining Infrared spectral data for the prepared samples. Absorption was also estimated ” 500 – 4000 cm−1, using 15 points smoothing, 4 cm−1 resolution and 64 scanning times. “Jeol‐Ex‐300 NMR spectrometry, JEOL – Japan” was used for obtaining“ nuclear magnetic resonance spectra, 1H NMR and 13C NMR” for the prepared samples.Photoluminescence for PDs and PDs@fabric was examined with “Spectro-fluorometer, JASCO FP8300″.

The synthesized PDs@fabric were employed in monitoring of metal ions due to their luminescent sensitivity. Different metal ions were selected in the present study represented in PbCl2, HgCl2, AuCl3, TiCl4, Cu(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2 and Cd(NO3)2. The metal salts were used with 25 mM and the detection was performed at room temperature. Strips from PDs@fabric were dipped in a certain metal salt for 2 minutes, then air dried and the fluorescence emission was measured by using spectro-fluorometer (JASCO FP8300) at excitation wavelength of 370 nm. For detection of gold ions, whereas, different concentrations in the range of 5 – 60 mM were prepared.

Acrylate wastes were currently exploited for clustering of PDs via infrared assisted technique. With magnetic stirring, acrylate wastes macromolecular chains were formerly hydrolyzed to low molecular weighted fragments. Consequently, clustering of PDs was performed via re-polymerization, aromatization and oxidation under the effect of infra-red irradiation [65]. Dialysis was performed for obtaining ultra-purified PDs to obtain highly-dispersed PDs with regulated topographical characters. The color of samples was changed from white to turbid yellowish color after PDs nucleation. After centrifugation and dialysis, the color eventually changed to pale yellow.

Fig. 1 showed TEM micrographs, histograms and size distribution for the prepared PDs via exploitation of acrylate wastes, to inform about the topographical and geometrical features of the clustered PDs. The represented micrographs in Fig. 1 a strongly affirmed the affinity of acrylate wastes in clustering of size/shape-controlled PDs with size distribution of 5.6 ± 2.1 nm. Zetasizer data plotted in Fig. 1 b also showed that 38 % of particles were exhibited with mean size of 8.7 nm. On the other hand, zeta electric potential was currently examined for the prepared colloidal sample of PDs for identification of the zeta potential, zeta-potential is a key parameter for the repulsive or attractive action between the nano-colloidal particles. The smaller is the suspended nanoparticles, the lower is the evaluated -ve value of zetapotential, i.e., lower is the stability of the nano-colloidal particles, meaning that, the highly dispersed PDs are protected from agglomeration or coagulation. From Fig. 1 c, the detected value of zetapotential was negative charged for the prepared nano-colloidal solution, as −6.5 mV to be advantageously exhibited with physical stabilization [66].

UV–Vis. spectroscopic results for the PDs samples that were nucleated from acrylate wastes were presented in Fig. 2 . From the spectral mapping in Fig. 2 a, it could be depicted that, PDs colloids of acrylate wastes were shown with strong a sharp peak at 270 nm that is characterized for π - π* transition (conjugation in aromatic clusters) and electron radiative relaxing in a broad shoulder at 370 nm with long extension in visible range that is corresponding to n- π* (for decorative groups as oxygen containing surficial groups) [67,68]. Meanwhile, the clustered PDs are mainly composed of aromatic conjugated nuclei decorated with oxygen containing functional groups. Moreover, the optical properties of the as-nucleated PDs were investigated as it is numerously reported that, the optical character is a reflect of the decoration for the aromatic nuclei of PDs with heteroatom containing surface groups [65,69].

The photoluminescence was investigated via photoluminescent and fluorescent emission for PDs that were currently nucleated from acrylate wastes. Fig. 2 b represented with the spectrum of emission after exciting at 370 nm & 400 nm, and from the presented results, it could be declared that, detecting the bands of emission in UV–Visible range can approve the composition of PDs with mobile pi- and non-bonding electrons on the aromatic nuclei, decorated with heteroatoms containing groups. The decoration of PDs surface is basically affected on the location of absorption peaks, that in turn resulting in the coloration of PDs colloids to endow their feasible colorimetry detection. In addition, excitation at 370 nm resulted in significant sharpening for the emission band that is detected in the visible range (440 nm, green region) from 6000 to 9000. Photoluminescence of PDs as affirmed from the above-discussed data could declare that, the ingrained PDs characterized with a significant optical character in visible light, to sequentially facilizing their exploitation in sensing to elaborate through the optical spectra of PDs. Fig. 2 c&d is plotted in order to study the photo-stability of the synthesized PDs through measurement UV–Vis absorbance and fluorescence emission for the prepared sample before and after it was kept for 4 months in daylight. The data revealed that the spectral data for PDs were not significantly changed even after exposure to daylight for 4 months to affirm their photo-stability for prolonged time of storing.

Fig. 3
a showed FT-IR spectra for acrylate wastes & PDs, whereas, it could be observed that, acrylate wastes were exhibited with characteristic peaks at 2922–2855 cm−1, 2240 cm−1, 1718 cm -1, 1448–1366 cm−1, 1224 cm−1 and 1058 cm−1 that were corresponding to CH2 asymmetric aliphatic, C≡N, C = O of carboxylic acid, scissors and bending vibrations of CH2 and CHCO, OH bending of neighboring carboxyl group and alkoxy C-O, respectively. However, after exploitation of acrylate wastes as PDs generator, all the significant bands of acrylate wastes were observed with red shifting, and new bands were detected at 1658 cm−1 and 878 cm−1, that are characteristic for C = C aromatic ring stretching and sp2 C–H bending, respectively. These could be evident to approve the affinity of acrylate wastes for ingraining of PDs decorated with oxygen containing surficial groups.

Fig. 3 b represented XRD data for acrylate wastes before and after its exploitation in nucleation of PDs, and the figured-out data showed that, the acrylate wastes were estimated with two characteristic peaks at 2θ = 16.6 ° and 30.4°, whereas, after its functionalization in ingraining of PDs, one broad diffraction peak at 17.1° and five sharp diffractions characteristic for PDs at 2θ° = 24.9°, 37.5°, 43.8°, 64.2° and 77.4° were estimated.

NMR spectra were represented in Fig. 4
a for elucidation of mechanism and confirming of PDs clustering from acrylate wastes. Fig. 4 a showed 1HNMR for PDs nucleated from acrylate wastes, whereas, the spectrum showed the characteristic bands for PDs at 2–4 ppm, 5 ppm and 8 ppm, corresponding to protons of sp3 C–H, protons of O–H, proton bonded to C = O group and protons of sp2 or aromatic nuclei [68,70]. Moreover, Fig. 4 b represented13CNMR spectral data of PDs and it could be observed that, the characteristic bands for PDs were estimated at 30–40 ppm and 163 ppm, signified for sp3 carbons and C = C for aromatic or sp2 carbons, respectively.

In the current approach, the clustered PDs were immobilized within cationized cotton in order to prepare PDs@Fabric scaffold to be sequentially applicable as optical sensors for detection of gold in aqueous medium. Whereas, cationization of cotton was previously reported in literature to increase the affinity of fabric for successive immobilization of PDs [71].

FTIR spectral data were plotted in Fig. 5 for cationized cotton before and after successive immobilization of PDs, in order to show the effect of doping PDs on the chemical composition of cationized cotton. From the plotted results it could be depicted that, cationized cotton as a cellulosic supporting template is exhibited with its significant spectral bands at 3331 cm−1 “OH stretching)”, 2900 cm−1 “asymmetric aliphatic CH2″, 1642–1649 cm−1 ”stretching C = O“, 1400–1300 cm−1 ”sp3 C–H bending“ and 1250–1001 cm−1 ”glycosidic link, C-O, C-O-C, and C-OH bonds“ [64,71]. However, after doping of PDs within fabric matrix, in addition to all the significant cotton bands that were persisted with lower intensity, significant PDs peaks were also observed as were analyzed in Fig. 5 a. These could evidently approve the successive doping of the as-clustered PDs within cationized cotton fabric.

Geometry and elemental structure for cationized cotton after doping of the as-prepared PDs (PDs@Fabric) were investigated via SEM & EDX analysis to be presented within Fig. 6 . From the presented data, it could be depicted that, cationized cotton could be successfully applicable as a supporting scaffold for successive doping of PDs, whereas, by comparing between SEM microscopic images for cotton (Fig. 6 a) and cationized cotton (Fig. 6 b) with cationized cotton after successive immobilization of PDs (PDs@Fabric, Fig. 6 c), it could be observed that, PDs were well-uploaded on the surface fabric.

The practical application of the presented work is to perform easy, quite simple and precious/accurate detection of gold ions in aqueous media, as the numerous applications of gold have led to its aggregation as dangerous wastes such as gold-mining post-production wastes and electronic wastes, that containing leftover gold, resulting in water pollution to be harmful for living organisms. As gold is very reactive and potentially noxious for human being, as it can chemically interact with human biopolymers, especially proteins and DNA, to deteriorate kidneys, peripheral nervous system, and liver.

One of the promising applications of florescent PDs is to be exploited as a probe for estimation of metal ions in aquatic streams for evaluation of the water pollution and for bio-sensing, as it could be demonstrated as easily applicable and environment friendly marker for detection. The current approach investigated a new quenching fluorescence sensor (PDs@Fabric) that was easily prepared by successive doping of PDs that were formerly implanted from acrylate wastes. This PDs@fabric sensor has significant photophysical characters with high fluorescence emission, as it exhibits an excellent detection response and selectivity for distinguishing gold ions from the interfering ions in aqueous media, that make it demonstrated for further exploitation in biological and environmental systems.

According to literature, the optical sensing of PDs were mainly attributed to the passivation of surface with outer protecting shell containing heteroatoms [72]. Wu et al. affirmed that, the photoluminescence of PDs with its graphite based nuclei, is mainly owing to the surface passivation with decorative functional groups [73]. Meanwhile, PDs are mainly composed of carbogenic cores described as optical centers and outer surface with synergetic hybridized groups [74]. So, the optical properties of the immobilized PDs within cationized cotton are mainly attributed to the decorative groups with overlapping of π-π* and n-π* electronic transitions for the graphite-based PDs. Moreover, the surface passivation of PDs mainly leaded to alter the absorption band, resulting in clustering PDs with observable color to endow the feasible colorimetry detection.

Herein, the superiority of PDs@Fabric as florescence sensing scaffold for the detection of gold in aquatic environment was investigated, as the estimated results were presented in Figs. 7 & 8
. The term in Fig. 7 is intensity for florescence emission of PDs@Fabric (after exciting at 340 nm) was detected for different metal ions [Pb2+, Hg2+, Au3+, Ti4+, Cd2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+]. From all the analyzed metal ions, PDs@Fabric was exhibited with high sensitivity and selectivity to gold ions in the presence of the other interfering metal ions, whereas, florescence emission of PDs was significantly quenched with gold ions (25 mM) to be significantly lowered from 600 to 200.

Fluorescence quenching of gold ions with PDs@Fabric depending on gold concentration was investigated for approving the superiority of the as-prepared PDs@Fabric in the detection of gold in aqueous solution (Fig. 8). When gold ions concentration was increased, the intensity of florescence emission for PDs@Fabric was gradually decreased. This could be attributed to the chemical coordination between gold ions and the surface functional groups of PDs that were immobilized within the as-prepared PDs@Fabric as quenching florescence scaffold.

1-(I/I0) was evaluated and linearity with gold salt concentrations was figured out, and it could be demonstrated that, linearity to the gradual increasing of gold ions concentration, as significant quenching of florescence for PDs@Fabric was estimated by 5–60 mM for gold ions. The florescence of PDs@Fabric is shown to be highly sensible for the concentration of gold ions. The evaluated regression results affirmed the linearity between quenching florescence of PDs@Fabric & the concentration of gold ions and the quenching mechanism could be identified according to Stern–Volmer equation [69]. At high gold ions concentration (30–60 mM), the curve of 1-(I/I0) did not fit well to the Stern–Volmer relation (R2 = 0.8046). However, observable linearity (R2 = 0.9893) was shown at low concentrations of gold ions (5–30 mM). As the gold ions concentration increased, the line is gradually ascended up, to show the dynamic and static characteristics. For static quenching, the interaction between PDs and the quencher (gold ions) reflected in the generation of optically inactive complex. Whereas, for dynamic quenching, the energy or charge transferring between the quencher and the excited state of CQDs mainly resulted in fluorescence quenching. In summarization, the estimated linear equation inserted in Fig. 8 c can be highly applicable for detecting gold ions at quite low concentration via informing the direct florescence intensity of PDs@Fabric or from the estimated values of 1-(I/I0).

Compared to the previous reports in literature, the optical characters of PDs with S- and/or N- containing surface functional groups were sensible for cuprous and ferric ions [75,76], whereas O-containing PDs were recently applied in detection of zinc and mercury [69]. Meanwhile, fluorescent sensor containing propargyl group was synthesized for detection of gold ion [77]. In the current approach, the prepared quenching florescence scaffold (PDs@Fabric) that containing optically active PDs decorated with oxygen and nitrogen groups is sensitive for gold ions. The variability in sensation of the quenching florescence of quantum dots (PDs) against metal ions is attributed to the decorative groups in its outer shell [78] and consequently attributing to the chemical interaction of metal ions with the surface groups of the exploited PDs. The significance of the presented work compared to the above mentioned works in literature for metal detection can be decided in its high applicability, whereas, the prepared PDs@fabric can be used in the form of dipstick for quite easy detection of gold ions in aquatic samples.

In agreement to literature [72], the quenching florescence mechanism of for the prepared PDs@Fabric can be elucidated as follows; the photoluminescence correlated excitation was suggested to be attributed to the aromatic conjugation, exciting of carbon, emission traps, surface passivation, and free zig-zag sites. Nitrogen and oxygen based-functional groups on the surface of PDs that were immobilized within fabric matrix could form intermolecular and intra-molecular H-bonding, for endowing the PDs with surface passivation. The chemical coordination between gold ions and the prepared florescence PDs@Fabric via the surface functional groups, produces stable complex, resulting in altering the physical/chemical characters of the florophores, as anisotropy and the intensity of fluorescence decreases [72]. Thus, ON–OFF turn of optical state was basically altered with addition of gold ions, attributing to the non-radiative electron transferring from excited states to the d-orbitals of gold ions. Production of the non-optically active coordination complex between PDs and gold ions gives a non-fluorescent “OFF” states.

The current study demonstrates easy, ecofriendly and cost saving method for clustering of florescent PDs from acrylate wastes without any of toxic chemicals. The synthesized PDs were successively immobilized within cationized cotton fabrics that acted as a supporting scaffold to prepare PDs@Fabric as a promising florescent dipstick to be easy applicable for effective, sensible, quick and selective florescent estimation of gold ions to be furtherly commercialized with accurate and satisfactory results. Herein, an investigative methodology is demonstrated for exploitation of acrylate wastes for clustering of florescent PDs to be sequentially exploited as optical sensors for detection of gold ions. The currently reported methodology can be described as a simple and environmentally benign technique for reusing of acrylate wastes to prepare fluorescent PDs. According to our knowledge, no researching reports in literature were concerned with affirming the affinity of PDs ingrained from acrylate wastes to be exploitable as optical sensors for detection of gold ions. In addition to, nucleation of PDs is successively performed without using any of toxic solvents, to be biologically suitable for application in sensitive detection potency. Clustering of PDs via the exploitation of acrylate wastes was approved via different instrumental characterizations like, UV–Visible spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy, FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. Moreover, PDs were successively immobilized within cationized cotton to prepare PDs@Fabric to be easily applicable as quenching florescence scaffold for detection of gold ions in aquatic media. The as-prepared PDs@Fabric was well characterized via SEM, EDX & FTIR, whereas, its affinity to be applicable as quenching florescence scaffold was approved via systematically studying and it was approved that, it exhibits an excellent detection response and selectivity for distinguishing gold ions from the interfering ions in aqueous media, that make it demonstrated for further exploitation in biological and environmental systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declares that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Singha, D. Kim, H. Seo, S.W. Cho, K.H. Ahn, Fluorescence sensing systems for gold and silver species, Chemical Society Reviews 44(13) (2015) 4367-4399.

D.S. Salem, M.A. Sliem, M. El-Sesy, S.A. Shouman, Y. Badr, Improved chemo-photothermal therapy of hepatocellular carcinoma using chitosan-coated gold nanoparticles, Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology 182 (2018) 92-99.

X. Yuan, Y. He, G. Zhou, X. Li, A. Feng, W. Zheng, Target challenging-cancer drug delivery to gastric cancer tissues with a fucose graft epigallocatechin-3-gallate-gold particles nanocomposite approach, Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology 183 (2018) 147-153.

G. Ramírez-García, M.Á. Honorato-Colin, E. De la Rosa, T. López-Luke, S.S. Panikar, J. de Jesús Ibarra-Sánchez, V. Piazza, Theranostic nanocomplex of gold-decorated upconversion nanoparticles for optical imaging and temperature-controlled photothermal therapy, Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry 384 (2019) 112053.

M.W. Akram, F. Raziq, M. Fakhar-e-Alam, M.H. Aziz, K. Alimgeer, M. Atif, M. Amir, A. Hanif, W.A.
